skill.name: raindrop-io
skill.description: Manage Raindrop.io bookmarks through an AI client connected to the official Raindrop.io MCP server. The skill provides setup guidance, bookmark workflows, search patterns, reading-list management, and research organization practices.
skill.summary: Organize, search, and manage Raindrop.io bookmarks with AI-assisted MCP workflows.
skill.author: DanSega1

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns, but review showed most are Markdown examples, product names, links, or setup documentation. The confirmed risk is legitimate but meaningful: users configure an AI client to reach the external Raindrop.io MCP endpoint and may expose or modify personal bookmark data. No prompt-injection attempt or confirmed malicious behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: External MCP Connection Handles Personal Bookmark Data
medium_findings.description: The skill directs users to connect an AI client to the official Raindrop.io MCP endpoint. This is expected for the skill, but it gives the AI client network access to authorized bookmarks, collections, tags, and highlights.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network endpoint and authentication model are directly documented. The behavior is legitimate for this skill, but the privacy impact is clear because bookmark account data is in scope.

medium_findings.title: Client Configuration Launches External MCP Bridge
medium_findings.description: Setup examples configure AI clients to run npx mcp-remote or node with mcp-remote against the Raindrop.io MCP URL. This is not executed by the skill itself, but users who follow the guide will run external package code in their client environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external command configuration is explicit and repeated in setup documentation. Risk is moderated because this is normal MCP setup guidance and not hidden execution inside the skill.

medium_findings.title: Inline API Token Example Requires Secret Handling
medium_findings.description: The setup guide shows a personal API token placed in MCP configuration as an environment value. The guide warns users not to commit tokens, but copied configurations can still expose credentials if shared or stored insecurely.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token handling pattern is clearly present and the document includes mitigation guidance. It is a handling risk rather than evidence of credential theft.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Trigger Static Command Heuristic
low_findings.description: The numerous Ruby or shell backtick detections are Markdown code fences and conversation examples, not executable Ruby code. Review found no script file or hidden command runner in the scanned files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown fences, natural language examples, or parameter lists. No evidence found of actual Ruby backtick execution or shell execution embedded in the skill.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Findings Are Documentation Paths
low_findings.description: The filesystem findings are setup documentation for client configuration paths and relative documentation links. No code reads or writes hidden files, and the path traversal detections are relative links back to local documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are installation instructions and Markdown links. No evidence found of runtime filesystem access or path traversal behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts appear to be triggered by words such as API, OAuth, MCP, AI, or product names in documentation. Review found no implementation of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe clients, OAuth, and the Raindrop.io MCP service rather than cryptographic code. No evidence found of weak algorithm use in the instruction files.

dangerous_patterns.title: npx mcp-remote Client Bridge
dangerous_patterns.description: The recommended MCP configuration launches npx with mcp-remote and a remote Raindrop.io MCP URL. Users should trust the package source and understand that this bridges their AI client to an external service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is explicitly documented. It is expected for MCP remote setup, but it is still an external command and network bridge.

dangerous_patterns.title: Personal Token in Configuration Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes an example token environment value in MCP configuration. Users must replace the placeholder and avoid sharing or committing the resulting file.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example and mitigation are both visible in the setup guide. The risk depends on user handling of the generated configuration.

content.value_statement: Large bookmark collections become hard to search, tag, and maintain. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and MCP-compatible clients structured workflows for Raindrop.io bookmark management.
actual_capabilities: Guides setup for the official Raindrop.io MCP server in Claude, Claude Code, ChatGPT, Cursor, Windsurf, VS Code, and Zed.
Provides workflows to save, search, update, delete, tag, and move bookmarks through connected MCP tools.
Documents collection, tag, highlight, search, and bulk-operation patterns for Raindrop.io accounts.
Supports reading-list workflows, research project setup, tag consolidation, and bookmark cleanup planning.
Explains OAuth and personal token setup options with security guidance for token handling.
limitations: Requires a Raindrop.io Pro subscription and a configured MCP-compatible AI client.
Depends on the official Raindrop.io MCP server, which the documentation describes as beta.
Does not include executable skill code or a bundled Raindrop.io client library.
Can only manage data authorized through the user account and available MCP tools.

use_cases.title: Research Source Management
use_cases.description: Create project collections, save sources, apply consistent tags, and retrieve highlights for reports or notes.

use_cases.title: Developer Reference Library
use_cases.description: Organize documentation, tutorials, GitHub links, and troubleshooting references by language, framework, project, and status.

use_cases.title: Reading List Cleanup
use_cases.description: Review unread articles, tag completed items, move outdated bookmarks, and keep personal reading queues manageable.

prompt_templates.title: Save a Bookmark
prompt_templates.prompt: Save this URL to my Raindrop.io account: [URL]. Ask me which collection, tags, and notes to use before making changes.

prompt_templates.title: Find Relevant Sources
prompt_templates.prompt: Search my Raindrop.io bookmarks for [topic]. Filter by [collection or tag] if available, and show the most relevant results first.

prompt_templates.title: Clean Up Tags
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my tags for duplicates or inconsistent names. Suggest merges first, then wait for approval before changing anything.

prompt_templates.title: Build Research Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Raindrop.io workflow for [project]. Propose collections, tags, review steps, highlight usage, and recurring cleanup actions.

output_examples.input: Save this article to my reading list and tag it for Python learning.
output_examples.output: The assistant asks for confirmation, saves the link to the Reading List collection, applies relevant tags, and summarizes the saved bookmark.

output_examples.input: Find my machine learning papers from last month.
output_examples.output: The assistant searches matching bookmarks, groups results by collection or tag, and offers to show highlights or refine the search.

output_examples.input: Help clean up old bookmarks in my Web Development collection.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies stale or untagged items, proposes tag updates or archive moves, and waits for approval before bulk changes.

best_practices: Use OAuth when possible, and keep personal API tokens out of shared files and repositories.
Confirm destructive actions such as bookmark, tag, highlight, or collection deletion before applying changes.
Use consistent lowercase tags and review collections on a recurring schedule.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real API tokens into chat unless your client and workspace are trusted.
Do not run bulk updates or deletes without first reviewing the affected bookmark list.
Do not rely on this skill without verifying that the Raindrop.io MCP tools are connected and authorized.

faq.question: Does this skill include executable code?
faq.answer: No. It is an instruction-based skill with setup guides, workflows, and reference documentation.

faq.question: Does it require a Raindrop.io Pro account?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation states that the official Raindrop.io MCP server requires a Pro subscription.

faq.question: Which AI clients are covered?
faq.answer: The guides mention Claude, Claude Code, ChatGPT, Cursor, Windsurf, VS Code, Zed, and other MCP-compatible clients.

faq.question: Can it delete bookmarks?
faq.answer: Yes, if the connected MCP tools support deletion and the user authorizes the action. Confirm deletion before proceeding.

faq.question: Where is bookmark data stored?
faq.answer: The skill says bookmarks remain in the user Raindrop.io account and are accessed through the authorized MCP connection.

faq.question: What is the main security consideration?
faq.answer: The main risk is connecting an AI client to an external MCP server that can access authorized bookmark data.
